title: Pair Sum to Target
statement:
You are given an array of integers **sorted in ascending order** and a target value. Return the 1-indexed positions of the two numbers that add up exactly to the target, as a two-element array `[i, j]` with `i < j`. You may assume exactly one valid pair exists, and you may not use the same element twice.

Your solution should run in O(n) time using the two-pointer technique — an O(n^2) brute force will time out on the hidden tests.

examples.explanation: nums[0] + nums[1] = 2 + 7 = 9, so the 1-indexed positions are 1 and 2.

title: Longest Window With K Fruit Types
statement:
A row of fruit trees is represented as an array of strings, each a fruit type (e.g. `"A"`, `"B"`). You're carrying baskets that can hold **at most `k` distinct fruit types** at once (unlimited quantity of each type). Picking must be done on a contiguous run of trees. Return the length of the **longest contiguous window** that contains at most `k` distinct fruit types.

An empty input array should return 0. Aim for O(n) using the sliding window technique.

examples.explanation: The window ["A","A","B"] (last 3 trees) has 2 distinct types and length 3; no longer window with <=2 types exists.
The whole list has only 1 distinct type.

title: Group Words by Letter Signature
statement:
Two words share a **letter signature** if one is a rearrangement of the other's letters (an anagram). Given an array of lowercase words, group words that share a signature together.

To make the output checkable, use this exact ordering contract:
- Each group (inner list) must be sorted alphabetically.
- The groups themselves (outer list) must be sorted by their first element, alphabetically.

examples.explanation: "eat","tea","ate" share letters e,a,t; "tan","nat" share a,n,t; "bat" is alone.
A single word is its own group.

title: Valid Bracket Sequence
statement: Given a string containing only the characters `(`, `)`, `[`, `]`, `{`, `}`, determine if it is valid. A string is valid if every opening bracket has a matching closing bracket of the same type, in the correct order (properly nested, not just balanced counts). An empty string is valid.

examples.explanation: Every bracket closes in the correct order.
'(' is closed by ']', which doesn't match.

title: Balanced Binary Tree Check
statement:
Given the root of a binary tree, determine if it is **height-balanced**: for every node in the tree, the heights of its left and right subtrees differ by no more than 1.

The tree is built for you from a level-order array (with `null` for missing children, LeetCode-style) — **do not redefine `TreeNode`**, it's provided in the execution environment with fields `val`, `left`, `right`. Implement `isBalanced(TreeNode root)`.

Aim for O(n) — a naive approach that recomputes height from scratch at every node is O(n^2) and may time out on deep trees.

examples.explanation: Every subtree's left/right height differs by at most 1.
The left subtree is 2 levels deeper than the right at some node.

title: Count Connected Islands
statement: Given a 2D grid of `0`s (water) and `1`s (land), an **island** is a group of `1`s connected horizontally or vertically (not diagonally). Return the number of distinct islands.

examples.explanation: A 2x2 block top-left, a single cell in the middle, and a 2-cell block bottom-right.
Three isolated single-cell islands separated by water.

title: Maximum Non-Adjacent Sum
statement: Given an array of non-negative integers, choose a subset of elements such that **no two chosen elements are adjacent** in the array, maximizing their sum. Return that maximum sum. An empty array has a maximum sum of 0.

examples.explanation: Take 2 + 9 + 1 = 12 (indices 0, 2, 4).
Take the first and last elements: 5 + 5 = 10.

title: Maximum Non-Overlapping Meetings
statement: You're given an array of meeting intervals `[start, end]` (a meeting occupies the half-open range from `start` up to `end`; meetings that touch at an endpoint, e.g. `[1,2]` and `[2,3]`, do **not** conflict). Return the maximum number of meetings you can attend if you can only attend one at a time.

examples.explanation: Attend [1,3], [3,5], [6,8] -- three non-overlapping meetings.
Just the one meeting.

title: First True Threshold
statement: You're given an array of booleans that is **sorted** in the sense that all `false` values come before all `true` values (e.g. `[false, false, true, true]`). Return the index of the first `true` value, or `-1` if there are none. Solve it in O(log n) using binary search on the boolean predicate — don't just scan linearly.

examples.explanation: Index 3 is the first true.
No true values exist.

title: Combinations Summing to Target
statement:
Given an array of **distinct** positive integers `candidates` and a target, return all unique combinations where the chosen numbers sum to `target`. The same number may be reused an unlimited number of times.

Ordering contract for deterministic checking: each combination must be sorted ascending, and the outer list of combinations must be sorted lexicographically.

examples.explanation: 2+2+3=7 and 7=7 are the two combinations.
Only 1+1=2 works.
